caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
a: Highly configured architectural systems and standard residential lines have materially different information and production labor mixes.
rho: Certification and energy-performance evidence establishes constraints and product complexity, not observed AI implementation.
rho: Smaller manufacturers may lack clean configuration and warranty data.
e: Eligibility depends on the boundary between repeat configured manufacturing and outsourced service.
e: The provided firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE and may include product-only or nontransferable operations.
s5: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
s5: A strategic plant purchase may not preserve the standalone firm or its recurring service relationships.
q: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
q: Retention differs markedly between specified custom curtain wall and standard residential products.
d5: Housing starts are only one demand component and do not measure metal product share.
d5: Interest rates, commercial vacancies, tariffs, glass availability, codes, and incentive policy can materially move demand.
o: Production can consolidate into larger manufacturers outside the lens.
o: Self-service configuration may reduce office labor without eliminating accountable manufacturing.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Quoted per-unit, package, or project revenue through dealer, contractor, and specification channels, with configuration, engineering, fabrication, testing documentation, warranties, and delivery often bundled.
lens.excluded_activities: Installation contractors, distributors, wood-window manufacturers, automotive trim producers, captive internal plants, firms outside the EBITDA band, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market metal window, door, frame, screen, trim, and curtain-wall manufacturers that repeatedly configure, engineer, fabricate, glaze, assemble, finish, and supply custom products to external dealers, contractors, architects, and building owners.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broad NAICS 332 occupation mix rather than wage-weighted tasks in metal window and door firms. The range maps exposure to quoting, configuration, engineering, takeoff, CAD, order entry, scheduling, purchasing, quality-image review, documentation, forecasting, and customer service while assigning low exposure to extrusion and sheet handling, cutting, machining, welding, glazing, sealing, assembly, finishing, packaging, maintenance, and physical testing; broader metal manufacturing may overstate machining and understate glazing and configuration work.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was located.
Highly configured architectural systems and standard residential lines have materially different information and production labor mixes.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across at least fifteen manufacturers, stratified by residential, commercial door, custom architectural, and curtain-wall operations, with current automation mapped across office, engineering, line, quality, maintenance, and logistics roles.
a.rationale: Product configuration and order-to-production data provide a meaningful AI surface, but BLS shows fabricated-metal employment concentrated in assemblers, welders, machinists, and machine operators. Physical glazing, sealing, hardware assembly, finishing, testing, and material handling remain difficult to substitute.

d5.bridge: Census housing starts measure new residential units rather than replacement windows and doors, commercial construction, metal share, curtain walls, or eligible LMM suppliers. Multifamily mix and regional framing materials can weaken the relationship, while renovation and energy upgrades make starts an incomplete downside proxy.
caveats: Housing starts are only one demand component and do not measure metal product share.
Interest rates, commercial vacancies, tariffs, glass availability, codes, and incentive policy can materially move demand.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year unit forecasts split among residential replacement, new single-family, multifamily, commercial doors, and curtain wall, with material share, building-code adoption, renovation spending, shipment data, backlog, dealer sell-through, and import volumes.
d5.rationale: Demand combines cyclical new construction with a large replacement and renovation base. Census reported May 2026 housing starts at a 1.177 million annual rate, down 8.7% from May 2025, indicating current new-build weakness. ENERGY STAR reports up to 13% average household energy-bill savings from replacing single-pane windows, supporting continued upgrade demand, though substitution from vinyl, wood, fiberglass, and imports constrains metal-specific growth.

caveats: Eligibility depends on the boundary between repeat configured manufacturing and outsourced service.
The provided firm count is a margin-bridged ESTIMATE and may include product-only or nontransferable operations.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level screen of the 279 estimated candidates covering private-label or contract revenue, repeat-customer share, custom engineering and configuration scope, external versus affiliate sales, normalized EBITDA, owner dependence, certifications, warranty claims, and plant condition.
e.rationale: Most firms sell manufactured products rather than a pure outsourced service. Custom architectural, private-label, contract-manufacturing, and configure-to-order firms with recurring external accounts can function as accountable outsourced suppliers, but standard branded-product sellers, captive plants, project-only operators, and businesses outside normalized EBITDA should be excluded.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332321 ($4,509,143,844) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332321 ($19,600,991,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $331,277 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332321); class <5: 254 firms, avg revenue/firm $656,878 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $128,879 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,808,035 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $354,736 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 131 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,767,748 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $739,232 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 196 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,158,173 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,189,234 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 83 firms, avg revenue/firm $46,275,711 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,079,294 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $253,082,680 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $49,654,822 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 279

caveats: Production can consolidate into larger manufacturers outside the lens.
Self-service configuration may reduce office labor without eliminating accountable manufacturing.
o.change_evidence: Dealer and contractor procurement data measuring shifts to imported finished units, vertically integrated installers, fully self-configured platforms, and lights-out plants, together with certification, testing, and warranty accountability.
o.rationale: Remaining demand requires an accountable operator to configure products for openings, climate, code, fire, security, and structural requirements; procure materials; fabricate and glaze; test and document performance; coordinate delivery; and honor warranties. Software can automate selection and ordering but cannot alone manufacture and certify the physical product.

caveats: No representative contract or pass-through dataset was located.
Retention differs markedly between specified custom curtain wall and standard residential products.
q.change_evidence: Matched SKU-, project-, dealer-, and contractor-level margin bridges before and after automation, including aluminum, steel and glass costs, rebates, discounts, bid hit rate, price-book resets, warranty claims, lead time, volumes, and competitor responses.
q.rationale: Configured project quotes, performance certification, brand, dealer service, warranties, and reliable lead times allow some retention. Competitive bids, distributor and contractor buying power, product comparisons, annual price books, raw-material escalators, rebates, and private-label tenders pass gains through. Reduced claims and faster configuration should retain better than visible factory labor savings.

caveats: Certification and energy-performance evidence establishes constraints and product complexity, not observed AI implementation.
Smaller manufacturers may lack clean configuration and warranty data.
rho.change_evidence: Two-year pre/post deployment evidence for AI configuration, estimating, CAD assistance, scheduling, machine vision, predictive maintenance, certification documentation, forecasting, and service, reporting labor, scrap, rework, claims, lead time, and overrides.
rho.rationale: Digital catalogs, drawings, quotes, orders, cut lists, machine data, inspection images, certification records, and warranties support AI implementation within five years. Constraints include fragmented dealer inputs, legacy configurators and ERP, code and climate-zone variation, NFRC and ENERGY STAR testing, product liability, custom hardware, cybersecurity, and the cost of dimensional, water-infiltration, or seal failures.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of US employer-business owners across industries planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Discounting plans for internal transfers, closures, failed transactions, and asset-only sales yields the base; mature private manufacturing ownership may make the general proxy conservative, while branding, certification, warranty, cyclicality, and dealer concentration can impede transfer.
caveats: Gallup is not industry- or size-specific and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
A strategic plant purchase may not preserve the standalone firm or its recurring service relationships.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of NAICS 332321 closed deals, plant sales, closures, and ownership changes from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, fenestration brokers, SBA loans, UCC records, and state registries, matched to eligibility and owner age.
s5.rationale: A fragmented private supplier base should generate succession and strategic acquisitions, but going-concern transfers require transferable channels, management, product approvals, warranties, working capital, equipment, and customer relationships.

principal_driver: Configured-to-order products create recurring digital work from specification and quote through production and certification.
principal_weakness: Much of the industry is product manufacturing, and physical assembly plus competitive channels constrain substitution and retention.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as manufacturing metal-framed windows, metal doors, frames, screens, molding and trim, and curtain walls, typically using purchased glass.
sources.title: NAICS 332321 Metal Window and Door Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 2025 broader-subsector employment including 125,250 assemblers, 112,520 welders, 101,640 machinists, and 56,060 cutting and press-machine operators.
sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332

sources.supports: Reports average household energy-bill savings up to 13% from replacing single-pane windows and describes climate-zone performance requirements, independent certification, NFRC labels, installation, and warranties.
sources.title: Residential Windows, Doors, and Skylights

sources.supports: Fall 2024 US survey found 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while 5% planned closure and 5% had no plan.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports May 2026 privately owned housing starts at a seasonally adjusted annual rate of 1.177 million, 8.7% below May 2025, and building permits at 1.413 million.
sources.title: Monthly New Residential Construction, May 2026

title: Metal Window and Door Manufacturing
